title: Kasha
subtitle: Russian buckwheat porridge with butter
excerpt: Roasted buckwheat groats cooked in water or stock, finished with butter. Russia's most fundamental grain dish — earthy, nutty, and deeply satisfying. Served as a side, a breakfast, or a simple main with a fried egg on top.
sourceNotes: Based on the kasha tradition documented across Russian culinary history, including references in Elena Molokhovets, 'A Gift to Young Housewives' (1861, public domain). Kasha (the word means both the dish and a general category of cooked grains in Russian) has been the staple grain preparation of the Russian table for centuries, predating the arrival of the potato.

content.text: Buckwheat kasha is the Russian equivalent of a plain grain side — like polenta to an Italian, or rice to a Japanese cook. The grain has a strong, nutty, slightly bitter flavour that pairs well with braised meats and mushroom sauces. In the morning it appears at breakfast, softened in milk and sweetened with butter and honey. At dinner it comes alongside beef stroganoff or slow-braised meat, as a platform for the sauce.

The ingredient for this recipe is whole buckwheat groats — the roasted, unhulled grain, sometimes labelled 'roasted buckwheat' or 'kasha' at Russian or Eastern European food shops. Buckwheat flour is a different product and will not work here. If you cannot find the whole groats, pearl barley cooked in the same way is a passable substitute in texture, though the flavour is milder.

The method follows a pilaf principle: toast the groats briefly in butter, add stock or water at a 1:2 ratio, bring to the boil, then steam covered on the lowest possible heat. The groats should absorb every drop of liquid and separate into individual grains — not a porridge, but a dry, fluffy mass.

items.prepNote: whole buckwheat groats, not flour — roasted groats labelled 'kasha'
optional: 1 egg beaten through the dry groats before cooking to keep grains separate

content.text: If using the egg method (it helps keep grains separate): beat 1 egg in a bowl, add the {{buckwheat-flour}} of dry buckwheat groats, and stir until every grain is coated. Spread the coated groats in a dry frying pan and toast over medium heat, stirring, for 3–4 minutes until dry and smelling nutty. Skip this step if making plain kasha.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a medium saucepan over a medium heat. Add the buckwheat groats and stir for 2 minutes until fragrant. Pour in {{stock-chicken}} of hot stock or water, add {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and stir once.

Bring to the boil, cover tightly with a lid, and reduce the heat to the lowest setting. Cook for 12–15 minutes. Do not lift the lid. When done, all the liquid should be absorbed. Remove from the heat and leave covered for 10 minutes. Fluff with a fork. Stir in a knob of butter and serve.

items.cause: Too much water, or lid not tight enough
items.fix: Use exactly 2 parts liquid to 1 part groats by volume. Make sure the lid sits properly. The egg-coating step helps keep grains separate.

items.cause: Not enough liquid or heat too low
items.fix: Add 50 ml more water, replace the lid, and cook for another 5 minutes.

items.cause: Using raw (unroasted) buckwheat rather than roasted groats
items.fix: Look for groats labelled 'roasted buckwheat', 'kasha', or with a deep brown colour. Pale greenish-white raw groats have a noticeably sharper flavour.

items.cause: Cooked in plain water rather than stock
items.fix: Use chicken or beef stock for a richer result. Alternatively, increase the butter at the end.

content.text: Kasha has been central to the Russian and Ukrainian diet for a thousand years. The proverb 'shchi da kasha — pishcha nasha' (cabbage soup and porridge are our food) captures the dish's place in Russian food culture: unpretentious, sustaining, and everywhere. Buckwheat arrived in Russia through trade routes from Central Asia around the thirteenth century and spread rapidly because it grows on poor soil, tolerates cold, and needs no processing beyond husking. Today it is sold pre-roasted in every Russian and Eastern European food shop. Served with butter, it remains unchanged from its historical form.
